coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for all-in-one detection and degradation: The role of photoinduced electron transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jiaoyan
Long, Caicheng
Zhang, Lei
Qing, Taiping
Zhang, Peng
Qing, Zhihe
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Using organic pollutants as natural electron transfer mediators and carbon dots (CDs) as photosensitizers for integrated monitoring and purification of contaminants is a new game-changing idea. We herein first reported a novel dual-functional platform of detection and degradation based on the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between tetracycline (TC) and CDs. On the one hand, CDs can be used as fluorescence probes for TC detection with good sensitivity and selectivity. On the other hand, they display an excellent photodegradation ability for TC without any external photosensitizer or cocatalyst. Studies suggested that the PET process is beneficial for the transfer of electrons from TC to CDs, which further accelerates electron transfer and suppresses the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the fluorescence quenching efficiency and photocatalytic activity. We believe this study can offer referable directions and theoretical guidance for the development of effective environmental pollution control and remediation technologies using CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalytic degradation
Fluorescence detection
Tetracycline
Photoinduced electron transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) HRTEM image of CDs. Inset: amplified crystal lattices. (c) Particle size distribution. (d) XRD pattern of CDs. (e) FTIR spectra for L-Cys and CDs. (f) Full XPS spectrum of CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, optimal excitation, and emission fluorescence profiles of CDs. Inset: Optical pictures of CDs solution under visible and UV light. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at incremental excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-Emission-Matrix spectrum of CDs. (d) Fluorescence time -scan spectrum of CDs within one hour.

Comparison of (a) Fluorescence response and (b) fluorescence intensities of CDs to different kinds of antibiotics. Fluorescence intensity variation of CDs in the presence of common coexisting substances, including (c) carbohydrates, amino acids, proteins, and (d) metal ions. (e) Fluorescence response of CDs to progressively increasing concentrations of TC. (f) Logarithm plot of F0-F/F0 versus the corresponding TC addition (inset: linear response range.).

(a) Photodegradation efficiency of the as-prepared CDs and the precursors for TC. (b) Photodegradation efficiency of different volume fractions of CDs on TC. (c) First-order kinetic fit plots and (d) kinetic rate constants for different CDs contents.

(a) The schematic of HOMO-LUMO molecular orbital energy level for TC. (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves for CDs before and after addition of TC. (c) Deduced mechanisms of photoluminescence and fluorescence quenching of CDs nanomaterials, and the photoelectron transfer process with TC. ESR spin-trapping spectra of CDs exhibiting the signals of (d) h+-TEMPO, (e) •OH- DMPO and (f) •O2‾- DMPO. (g) Photocatalytic degradation rates of CDs after the addition of different scavengers. (h) Suggested mechanisms of RSs generation on the surface of the photocatalyst and its photocatalytic oxidation of TC.
(a) Diagrammatic drawing of CDs preparation process. (b) Application drawing of the role of CDs as an “all-in-one” nanomaterial for the determination and degradation of TC.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for all-in-one detection and degradation: The role of photoinduced electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resource & Environment and Safety Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan 411201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resource & Environment and Safety Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resource & Environment and Safety Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan 411201, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry, School of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry, School of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Cytochemistry, School of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using organic pollutants as natural electron transfer mediators and carbon dots (CDs) as photosensitizers for integrated monitoring and purification of contaminants is a new game-changing idea. We herein first reported a novel dual-functional platform of detection and degradation based on the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between tetracycline (TC) and CDs. On the one hand, CDs can be used as fluorescence probes for TC detection with good sensitivity and selectivity. On the other hand, they display an excellent photodegradation ability for TC without any external photosensitizer or cocatalyst. Studies suggested that the PET process is beneficial for the transfer of electrons from TC to CDs, which further accelerates electron transfer and suppresses the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the fluorescence quenching efficiency and photocatalytic activity. We believe this study can offer referable directions and theoretical guidance for the development of effective environmental pollution control and remediation technologies using CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported an approach for all-in-one detection and degradation of pollutants in water environment based on a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between tetracycline (TC) and carbon dots (CDs). PET process is beneficial for the transfer of an electron from TC to CDs, which efficiently accelerates electron transfer and inhibits the recombination process of electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the fluorescence quenching rate and photocatalytic activity. We believe that this study can offer referable directions and theoretical guidance for the development of effective environmental pollution control and remediation technologies using CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination induced by antibiotic residues has always been a serious issue because of its adverse impact on living organisms and ecosystems [1,2]. To address the problem, in addition to controlling pollution sources and reducing emissions, it is necessary to develop accurate detection and efficient removal methods. In recent years, a series of antibiotic residue monitoring and remediation strategies have been developed [3]. Many types of small molecules or nanomaterials have been designed as fluorescent sensors, such as organic small molecules [4], nanometal oxides [5], and metal-organic-complexes [6,7], for selective and specificity detection of the antibiotic residues in water [8]. Additionally, various catalysts, including carbon-based nanomaterials [9,10], metallic nanoparticles [11,12], and metal-organic frameworks [13,14], are being tested for the possible degradation of antibiotic residues [15]. However, most of the developed methods can only execute either the detection or the removal task respectively, which limits their further application in multiple environmental requirements.

In this scenario, carbon dots (CDs) have inspired growing interest for their excellent optoelectronic properties, ultrasmall size, abundant and inexpensive precursors, tunable band gaps, and ease of surface functionalization [16–18]. We envisioned that functionalized CDs could work as the new candidates for both detection and degradation of antibiotic residues. First, the change of intrinsic fluorescence intensity can act as the output signal for fluorescence analysis [19,20]. Second, the bottom-up synthesis strategy of organic precursors enables specific functional groups to be attached to the surface of fluorescent CDs. Thus, selective sensing for different applications can be achieved [21,22]. Third, CDs, as zero-dimensional semiconductor nanocrystals with excellent light-harvesting capability, strong photoelectric effect, and outstanding electronic conductivity, can possess favorable competition among photoelectric catalytic materials [23,24]. Fourth, CDs has photoinduced electron donor-acceptor property. For one thing, the excited fluorophore accepts electrons, leading to fluorescence quenching. For another, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process can promote the recombination annihilation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the efficient separation of photogenerated excitons, increasing the photocatalytic activity and stability of CDs. Given the intrinsic photoelectrical properties and unique catalytic performance of CDs, the following questions arise: Can CDs serve as a multifunction platform for “all-in-one” efficient detection and photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic residues? Unfortunately, to the best of our knowledge, no research has been reported that answers the question [25].

In this study, we report the first application of fluorescent CDs as a novel dual-functional platform for “all-in-one” determination and photodegradation of tetracycline (TC), which acts as the target contaminant and the natural electron transfer mediator. As a proof-of-concept, we prepared CDs through a one-step microwave-assisted thermal decomposition method [26]. The as-synthesized CDs could not only serve as fluorescence sensors for TC detection with splendid sensitivity and specificity but also exhibit significant photocatalytic degradation properties ( Scheme 1). Subsequently, according to the relevant theoretical calculation, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves and electron spin resonance analysis, the fluorescence response and photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of CDs for TC were systematically researched. Overall, this report confirms that the as-synthesized CDs have prospective applications in both fluorescence sensing and photodegradation of TC in the water environment. Simultaneously, it also enlightens new intellections and methods to develop multifunctional nanomaterials for determining and removing hazardous organic compounds.
All chemical reagents and utilized instrumentation were listed in the Text S1 and Text S2, respectively.

The preparation method has been improved based on previous studies[27]. Briefly, 0.1 g of L-cysteine (L-Cys) and 2 ml of phosphoric acid were uniformly dispersed in 8 ml of ultrapure water. The mixture was heated in a microwave reactor for 120 s (350 W), by which CDs could be synthesized rapidly without the need for complex equipment and additional surface passivation. Subsequently, the black viscous liquid obtained was diluted by adding 10 ml of ultrapure water. The primary product was then centrifuged (3000 rpm, 10 min) and ultrafiltered (0.22 µm) to further eliminate unreacted material. After that, the obtained dark brown solution was dialyzed (500–1000 Da) for 24 h and freeze-dried for 48 h to finally metamorphose into brown-black solid CDs, which were stored at 4 °C for subsequent applications.

The fluorescence response performance of CDs to TC was further evaluated. A certain volume of CDs stock solution was an addition to the TC solution at concentrations of 0.1–300 μM in a total volume of 500 μL. Then, after five minutes of incubation, the emission fluorescence spectra of CDs were obtained under optimal excitation conditions. Under the same procedures, the selectivity and anti-interference performance of this fluorescent sensor for TC was analyzed (Text S3).

The degradation experiments were carried out as follows. The CDs stock solution with different volumes was dispersed in 100 ml pure water containing specific concentrations of TC and stirred magnetically for 30 min in the dark to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium before light radiation. Subsequently, a 350 W xenon lamp was utilized as a sunlight simulator to rehearse the photocatalytic degradation process, and a circulating water bath was employed to maintain the reaction temperature at 25.0 ± 1 ℃. Whereafter, the residual concentrations of TC at fixed time intervals (0–60 min) were evaluated by measuring the maximum absorption at 363 nm through UV spectrophotometry. The degradation efficiency of TC was calculated according to the following formula (1); and the experimental data were fitted, and the degradation rate constants were evaluated according to the following quasi-first-order kinetics equation of photocatalytic reaction (2):
(1)
DR%=(C 0-C t)/C 0 ×100%
(2) ln (C/C 0) = kt
where C 0 is the initial concentration of TC, C t is the concentration of TC at different reaction time, k is the apparent rate constant of reaction, and t is the illumination time.

To investigate the role of free radical pathways in TC degradation, free radical trapping experiments and free radical scavenging experiments were performed (Text S4). To further determine the mechanism of detection and degradation of TC by CDs, theoretical calculations and decomposition pathway derivation were performed. The Gaussian 16 program was performed to obtain the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), frontier electron densities (FEDs), and the total charge for TC [28]. And according to the empirical formula, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CDs were evaluated [29]. The absorption wavelength of the valence band is calculated by the band gap.
(3)
E HOMO = -e(E ox+4·4)
(4)
E LUMO = -e(E red+4·4)
(5)
E HOMO = E LUMO-E g
(6)
λ≈ 1240/E g
where E ox and E red are the starting points of the redox potential of CDs respectively, and E g is the band gap measured by the UV-DRS absorption spectrum of CDs, λ is the peak wavelength of light.
The degradation pathway analysis regarding the catalytic mechanism was analyzed in Texts S5 of the Supporting Information.

To verify the successful preparation of CDs, the morphology and microscopic structures of the samples were characterized by HRTEM and EDS. The CDs exhibit good solution dispersion, and the average particle diameter is 3.2 ± 0.27 nm ( Fig. 1a and c), which is basically consistent with the AFM analysis (Fig. S1). In addition, the interplanar spacings of 0.21 nm correspond to the (100) crystal plane of the CDs (Fig. 1b) [30,31]. Meanwhile, EDS analysis determined that the CDs nanomaterials had a C: N: O: P: S molar ratio of 4.09: 1: 2.06: 1.19: 1.33 (Fig. S2).

The chemical structure and elemental composition of the CDs were examined. The XRD pattern of CDs manifests a dominant peak at 2θ = 23.8°, which can be assigned to the (002) reflection of carbon (Fig. 1d) [32]. The Raman spectra of the CDs exhibit two typical peaks of carbon materials (Fig. S3). The D band centered at 1326 cm−1 attributed to disordered sp3 hybridized carbon, while the G band located at 1569 cm−1 for crystalline sp2 hybridized carbon. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated as 1.01, which represents the as-prepared CDs have a certain graphitization degree [33–35]. There is no characteristic absorption peak of L-Cys identified in the FTIR spectrum of CDs, probably because L-Cys is bonded to the interior of CDs instead of the surface of CDs (Fig. 1e) [36]. XPS spectroscopy provides favorable evidence. Presented in Fig. 1f is the full XPS spectrum of the constituent elements. The CDs reveal a new typical peak, P 2p located at 133 eV from those of L-Cys due to the addition of H3PO4 as a co-reactant. Besides, the high-resolution spectra of the C 1 s, N 1 s, O1s, P 2p, and S 2p states were all analyzed (Fig. S4). Specifically, the C 1 s spectrum illustrates three peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.4 eV, which are attributed to the C-C, C-N, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum depicts two peaks at 399.5 and 401.2 eV, which are ascribed to the C-N-C and N-H bonds, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum is fitted by two peaks at 531.3 and 535.4 eV that can be assigned to the C-O/N-O and COO- bonds, respectively [37–39]. Consequently, all of the data above suggest that the CDs functioning with hydroxy and carboxy carbonyl are presumably derived from the carbon precursors of L-Cys.

After identifying the microscopic morphology, physical phase structure and elemental composition of CDs, the optical properties were also examined. The CDs show two absorption bands in the UV region at 282 nm and 387 nm ( Fig. 2a), which are attributed to the π-π * electronic transition of sp2 carbon clusters and the n-π * electronic transition of the surface oxygen-containing functional group CO, respectively [40]. To further explore the fluorescence properties of CDs, the fluorescence spectra of CDs at incremental excitation wavelengths were recorded in Fig. 2b and c. It can be noted that the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases, where the fluorescence emission is ascribed to the excitation-independent type. Furthermore, the maximum emission wavelength was 490 nm with the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Fig. 2d presented the fluorescence stability of CDs within a one-hour time scan, suggesting that the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change significantly within a certain retention time. The above experimental conclusions show that CDs have reliable photoluminescence properties and fluorescence stability, which can be taken advantage of as candidate materials for practical application.

Selectivity and anti-interference performance are crucial in examining the sensing characteristics of CDs detection methods. Fig. 3a and b depict the selectivity of the six antibiotics in CDs solution. TC exhibited an evident fluorescence quenching on CDs compared with other types of antibiotics. Meanwhile, the interference experiments of common metal ions, carbohydrates, and proteins in real water on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were carried out. As described in Fig. 3c and d, the majority of interfering substances had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs solutions. These experimental results elucidate that CDs can act as sensing devices for TCs with high specificity and good anti-interference performance. Whereafter, the detection sensitivity of CDs to TC concentration was explored, which is an important parameter for assessing the application potential of CDs. As illuminated in Fig. 3e, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the incremental concentration of TC. Notably, Fig. 3f reveals the logarithmic curve of the fluorescence quenching rate (F0-F/F0) versus TC concentration in the range of 0.01 μM∼100 μM, showing a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998). Simultaneously, the limit of detection determined by 3σ/S was 6.5 nM [41]. It can be judged that the as-prepared CDs have a wide dynamic range and low LOD, indicating the advantage of CDs in the ultrasensitive detection of TC. Compared with recently reported fluorescent detection methods for TC, this strategy based on the as-prepared CDs is superior or substantially equivalent to previous disquisitive techniques (Table S1).

In addition to assessing the detection ability, the degradation potential of CDs for TC was further investigated under light conditions. Fig. 4a shows the slight photodegradation of TC in the absence of CDs. However, TC was rapidly decomposed within the initial 10 min of light irradiation after the addition of CDs. The photocatalytic removal efficiency of TC is as high as 62.5% after 1 h of photocatalysis. Furthermore, the experimental results showed that the degradation efficiency of TC is related to the amounts of CDs. As elaborated in Fig. 4b-d, the experimental data on the photodegradation kinetics of TC are consistent with a pseudo-first-order reaction, with the degradation rate constant obtained from the slope of the fitted straight line. As the volume fraction of CDs was increased from 0% to 4%, the photodegradation rate constant of TC increased from 0.00027 min−1 to 0.01614 min−1, because more catalysts can provide more active sites. However, when the CDs content was further increased to 7%, the photodegradation rate constant decreased from 0.01614 min−1 to 0.00795 min−1. The situation may be due to the particle shading effect and internal filter effect in solution, which compete and inhibit photons from reaching the surface of the CDs [28]. Table S2 compares this method with the recently reported photocatalytic degradation of TC using a single material. This degradation procedure is functionally equivalent to the numerous methods that have been reported.

For a better understanding of the interaction mechanism between TC and CDs, the quenching mechanism was first discussed. As shown in Fig. S5, The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was shortened, and the energy gap between the fluorescence emission of photosensitizer and the UV absorption of the quencher suggest that fluorophore deactivation may be related to PET [42]. To verify the correctness of the conjecture, the relative energies of HOMO and LUMO for TC were first evaluated by the density functional theory (DFT) method at the B3LYP/6–311 G* level, which is − 6.10 eV and − 2.09 eV, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 5a. Then, cyclic voltammetry was used to estimate the electrochemical properties of CDs, and the Ered and Eg values were − 0.375 eV and 2.65 eV, respectively (Fig. S6). Based on these calculation data and empirical formulas (see support information for details), the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CDs were finally evaluated to be − 6.71 eV and − 4.06 eV, respectively. The above results suggest that it is feasible to achieve the transfer of electrons between the HOMO orbits of TC and CDs under light induction [43].

Subsequently, Fig. 5b displays the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence and presence of TC, exploring further the carrier recombination rate and electron transfer capability of the fluorescent nanomaterials. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs tested was 5.19 ns before the addition of TC, manifesting the high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Initially, the electrons in the ground state of the CDs absorb light energy and then transition to the excited state. with the end of the pulsed light signal, the photo-generated electron-hole (e−/h+) recombination occurs efficiently [44]. However, the carrier lifetime of CDs dropped to 3.86 ns after exposure to TC. The shortening phenomenon indicates dynamic quenching, confirming efficient electron transfer on the carrier transport layers. CDs-TC complexes were formed by PET, in which TC also absorbed light radiation to reach an excited state. the electrons were instantaneously transferred from the HOMO orbit of TC (electron donor) to the HOMO orbit of CDs (electron acceptor). The recombination of photogenerated carriers was annihilated, and the excited fluorophores of CDs were reduced, eventually leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5c) [45,46]. The photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectra (ESI) were used for further corroboration. As shown in Fig. S7, the CDs have high photocurrent signal value and low electrochemical impedance value, which correspond to efficient electron-hole separation and excellent electron conductivity of CDs [35,47]. Overall, the reductive-PET mechanism was validated, with fluorescent CDs acting as electron acceptors.

Generally, there are two primary pathways for TC under light irradiation: direct photolysis and sensitized photodegradation. The former manifests that antibiotic molecules decompose themselves after absorbing luminous energy [48–50]. The latter means that the substances in the aqueous environment absorbs photons to become excited molecules and then undergoes a redox reaction with water molecules to generate reactive species (RSs), which act on antibiotics to degrade into small molecular products [51,52]. Therefore, it was worth pondering the effects of free radicals and non-radical mechanisms on organic pollutants. On the one hand, PET accelerates the rapid electron transfer of TC towards CDs, which is beneficial to the self-decomposition of TC [53]. On the other hand, PET facilitates the effective separation of photoelectrons and holes of CDs, which is the critical influencing factor for the degradation of photocatalysts. At this point, the electrons trapped in the conduction band (CB) and the holes in the remaining valence band (VB) preserve the redox ability of CDs, promoting the photocatalytic activity and stability of CDs.

The electron spin resonance experiment using DMPO and TEMPO as radical trapping agents further explored the generation of RSs in the photocatalytic process. As depicted in Fig. 5d-f, there is no appreciable signal of h+, •OH or •O2‾ radicals in the darkness, while the evident and powerful characteristic signals for h+-TEMPO, •OH-DMPO, and •O2‾-DMPO are monitored under light irradiation. The results indicate that the introduction of CDs could generate h+, •OH, and •O2‾. To further determine the contributions of the different RSs in the TC degradation process for CDs, a quenching experiment was conducted using IPA, EDTA-2Na, and BQ as h+, •OH, and •O2‾ scavengers, respectively. The presence of IPA has a restraining function on TC breaking down (42.5%). In contrast, EDTA-2Na and BQ have a significant depressor influence on the disintegration of TC, with suppression efficiencies of up to 62.5% and 89.6%, respectively (Fig. 5g). These results reveal that •O2‾ plays critical role in the degradation of TC, which is consistent with the above analysis of the luminescence mechanism. Part of the holes in the VB of CDs are compounded with the electrons transferred from TC, resulting in a lessened dedication to the catalytic effect.

To verify the possible influence of RSs on the reaction system, UV-DRS and VB-XPS spectroscopy were utilized to demonstrate the energy band structure of the as-prepared CDs [54]. The band gap was estimated to be 2.65 eV (Fig. 8a), and the absorption wavelength could be calculated to be 468 nm, which indicated that CDs possess a wide range of light absorption and are beneficial for visible-light-driven photocatalysis. The VB maxima for CDs was determined to be located at 1.85 eV (Fig. S8b). Considering that the CB potential of CDs (−0.8 eV) was higher than the standard reduction potential of O2/•O2‾ (−0.33 eV vs. NHE). The electrons on the CB of CDs will reduce O2 to generate •O2‾ (O2 + e‾ → •O2‾), and partial •O2‾ can continue to interact with H+ to generate •OH under the electron reduction (•O2‾ + e‾ + 2 H+ → 2OH‾). In addition, the residual photogenerated h+ at the VB of CDs retained the strong redox ability, which could directly oxidize and degrade TC (Fig. 5h) [55,56]. Finally, TC is effectively attacked by RSs (•OH, •O2‾ and h+), decomposing to simpler intermediates and further mineralizing into CO2 and H2O (Fig. S9-S10).

This study first demonstrated that single CDs could not only be employed as a fluorescence sensing device for TC determination with splendid sensitivity and specificity but also exhibit appreciable photocatalytic degradation performance for TC without any external photosensitizer or cocatalyst. Possessing the unique nature of photoelectric performance and tunable functionality, CDs materials are new and ideal candidates with intrinsic “all-in-one” sensing and degrading functionalities. Furthermore, the intrinsic mechanisms of fluorescence response and photocatalytic decomposition of TC by CDs, as well as the role of PET processes in the interaction between CDs and TC were revealed in depth and detail from a theoretical perspective. The present study provides new insight into the application of CDs for “all-in-one” detection and degradation of organic contaminations in water. We believe that this paper can provide referable directions for the environmental application of CDs and provide theoretical guidance for the development of effective technologies in environmental pollution monitoring and purification.

Jiaoyan Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Caicheng Long: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lei Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Peng Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Taiping Qing: Writing – review & editing. Zhihe Qing: Writing – review & editing. Bo Feng: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20617, 21777135), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2022JJ50131, 2022JJ30559, 2021JJ30664), and Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province, China (20B585, 21B0129).
